label: Adelanto ICE Processing Center
sub: GEO Group-operated facility in California's High Desert. Repeatedly cited by DHS OIG for substandard medical care, improper solitary confinement, and multiple detainee deaths. Subject of numerous ACLU investigations.

label: Stewart Detention Center
sub: CoreCivic-operated facility in Lumpkin, Georgia — one of the largest ICE detention centers in the U.S. Multiple deaths reported; ACLU documented denial of adequate medical care and abuse by guards.

label: Eloy Federal Contract Facility
sub: CoreCivic-operated facility in Eloy, Arizona. Historically one of the highest death rates of any ICE detention facility; 15+ deaths documented between 2003 and 2015 prompting DHS investigations.

label: South Texas Family Residential Center (Dilley)
sub: CoreCivic-operated family detention facility with capacity of ~2,400. Opened in 2014 as the largest family detention center in the U.S., housing mothers and children seeking asylum. Subject of Flores litigation.

label: Karnes County Residential Center
sub: GEO Group-operated family detention center in Karnes City, Texas. Houses immigrant mothers and children. Multiple court challenges over conditions. Converted from all-male facility in 2014 during family detention expansion.

label: Krome North Service Processing Center
sub: ICE-owned facility in Miami, Florida. One of ICE's oldest facilities, operating since the 1980s. Criticized for overcrowding, medical neglect, and poor conditions throughout its history.

label: Port Isabel Service Processing Center
sub: ICE-owned facility in Los Fresnos, Texas, near the southern border. Long-standing facility used for adult single men. Served as a major processing hub during family separation crisis in 2018.

label: Buffalo Federal Detention Facility (Batavia)
sub: Federally operated ICE detention center in Batavia, New York. Handles immigration cases from upstate New York and Canada border region. Houses men and has operated since INS era.

label: El Paso ICE Processing Center
sub: Major ICE processing facility in El Paso, Texas. Averaged 2,954 detainees in February 2026 — highest single-facility population count. Major operations center during 2025 enforcement surge.

label: Bluebonnet Detention Facility
sub: ICE detention facility in Anson, Texas used in 2025 to house alleged Tren de Aragua members detained under the Alien Enemies Act. Site of significant legal battles over due process for Alien Enemies Act detainees.

label: Central Arizona Florence Correctional Center
sub: CoreCivic-operated multi-purpose correctional center in Florence, Arizona. Holds ICE detainees alongside federal prisoners. Part of Florence, Arizona's identity as a major ICE detention hub.

label: Winn Correctional Center
sub: LaSalle Corrections-operated facility in Winnfield, Louisiana that holds ICE detainees. Louisiana became a major hub for ICE detention during the Trump era due to favorable state contracts and lower state oversight.

label: Central Louisiana ICE Processing Center
sub: LaSalle Corrections-operated facility in Jena, Louisiana. Part of the concentration of ICE detention in rural Louisiana far from detainees' families and legal services.

label: Broward Transitional Center
sub: GEO Group-operated facility in Pompano Beach, Florida. Handles detainees from South Florida ICE field office. Multiple documented deaths and civil rights complaints filed with DHS Office of Civil Rights.

label: Sherburne County Jail
sub: County jail in Elk River, Minnesota contracted by ICE. Documented as holding detainees for the longest average duration — 259 days per Freedom for Immigrants. Far from major immigration courts and legal services.

label: Postville, Iowa — Agriprocessors Raid (2008)
sub: Site of the largest single-site worksite enforcement action in U.S. history at the time: 389 workers arrested at Agriprocessors kosher meatpacking plant on May 12, 2008. Most were Guatemalan and Mexican workers; dozens were convicted of federal identity fraud crimes.

label: Swift & Company Plant — Greeley, CO (2006)
sub: One of six Swift & Company meatpacking plants raided simultaneously on December 12, 2006 in Operation Wagon Train — the largest coordinated worksite enforcement action at the time. 1,297 workers arrested nationwide across six states.

label: Swift & Company Plant — Marshalltown, IA (2006)
sub: One of six Swift & Company meatpacking plants raided in Operation Wagon Train on December 12, 2006. Devastating impact on the Marshalltown community, which had a large Latino immigrant workforce.

label: Chicago — Operation Safe City (2017)
sub: Chicago was one of 10 sanctuary jurisdictions targeted in ICE's Operation Safe City in September 2017. The 4-day enforcement surge targeting sanctuary policies resulted in arrests across the metro area. Chicago's mayor denounced the operation.

label: Long Island, NY — MS-13 Enforcement Surge
sub: Long Island's Suffolk and Nassau Counties became a major focus of ICE's anti-MS-13 campaign in 2017-2018. ICE conducted repeated sweeps; reports emerged of immigrant youth falsely labeled as gang members, prompting civil rights investigations.

label: New York City — 2025 Enforcement Surge
sub: New York City was one of the largest targets of ICE's 2025 enforcement surge under Operation Axis. Thousands arrested in the metro area in January–February 2025. Mayor Adams declined to obstruct ICE operations, breaking with previous city policy.

label: Los Angeles — 2025 Mass Arrests
sub: Los Angeles was heavily targeted in the 2025 Trump enforcement surge. ICE conducted coordinated neighborhood operations; California Governor Newsom challenged the operations in court. Schools and churches saw ICE activity nearby, causing widespread fear.

label: Clint, TX — Border Processing Facility
sub: The Clint, Texas Border Patrol station became notorious in 2019 when reports emerged of severely overcrowded conditions and children being held without adequate food, water, or sanitation. Congressional delegations and advocates inspected the facility.

label: San Ysidro Port of Entry
sub: San Ysidro is the busiest land border crossing in the Western Hemisphere and a major site of ICE/CBP enforcement operations, asylum processing, and anti-immigration protests. Temporary closures occurred during the 2018 border surge.

label: McAllen, TX — Border Processing Hub
sub: McAllen and the Rio Grande Valley became the epicenter of the 2018 family separation crisis and subsequent asylum surges. Thousands of families were processed through McAllen facilities; the city's civic center was used as a temporary processing center.

label: Laredo, TX — Border Crossing
sub: Laredo, Texas hosts the busiest commercial port of entry on the U.S.-Mexico border. Major site of ICE enforcement operations and commercial vehicle inspections. Significant narcotics and human trafficking interdiction operations.

label: Eagle Pass, TX — Surging Border Crossing
sub: Eagle Pass became a flashpoint in 2023-2025 border debates. Texas National Guard deployed razor wire; federal courts repeatedly ruled on the state-federal conflict over border enforcement authority. Site of significant ICE operations.

label: Del Rio, TX — Haitian Migrant Crisis (2021)
sub: In September 2021, approximately 15,000 Haitian migrants gathered under the Del Rio International Bridge awaiting processing. Photos of Border Patrol agents on horseback appeared to use reins to deter migrants, triggering a national controversy. Most migrants were expelled under Title 42.

label: Nogales, AZ — Border Enforcement
sub: Nogales is a key Arizona border crossing and a major site of ICE and CBP operations. Family separation cases were processed through Nogales facilities in 2018. Significant drug and human trafficking interdiction operations throughout ICE history.

label: Brownsville, TX — Southern Border Enforcement
sub: Brownsville sits at the southern tip of Texas on the Rio Grande. Home to the federal court where Judge Andrew Hanen issued major rulings against Obama immigration policies. Major processing point for migrants crossing from Matamoros, Mexico.

label: U.S. Supreme Court — Washington, D.C.
sub: Site of landmark ICE-related rulings including Zadvydas v. Davis (2001), Jennings v. Rodriguez (2018), DHS v. Thuraissigiam (2020), United States v. Texas (2023), and Trump v. J.G.G. (2025) — each significantly shaping ICE detention and enforcement authority.

label: S.D. Cal. — Ms. L v. ICE (Family Separation Litigation)
sub: The U.S. District Court for the Southern District of California, where Judge Dana Sabraw presided over the landmark Ms. L v. ICE class action. Sabraw issued the June 2018 family reunification order and oversaw a historic 2023 settlement requiring ongoing remediation.

label: S.D. Tex. — Judge Hanen's Court
sub: U.S. District Court in Brownsville, Texas, where Judge Andrew Hanen repeatedly blocked Obama and Biden immigration policies. Hanen issued injunctions against DAPA (2015), expanded DACA, and Biden's enforcement priority guidance, making this court a central arena in immigration law.

label: D.D.C. — J.G.G. v. Trump Litigation
sub: The U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia where Judge James Boasberg issued the first TRO blocking Alien Enemies Act deportations to El Salvador in 2025. The Supreme Court later vacated Boasberg's order in a 5-4 ruling in Trump v. J.G.G.

label: Ninth Circuit — San Francisco
sub: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit has been a major venue for ICE-related litigation, ruling in cases involving Flores settlement compliance, sanctuary city protections, and DACA. The court issued multiple injunctions blocking Trump-era immigration policies.

label: DHS Headquarters — Washington, D.C.
sub: DHS Headquarters at St. Elizabeths Campus in Washington, D.C. Command center for ICE policy directives, enforcement priority memoranda, and major enforcement operations. Location of ICE's national leadership.

label: Portland, OR — 'Occupy ICE' Protests (2018)
sub: Portland became the center of the 'Occupy ICE' protest movement in June–July 2018, following the family separation crisis. Protesters blockaded the local ICE field office for nearly a month, preventing agents from entering. City officials initially declined to intervene; the encampment was eventually cleared by police.

label: Los Angeles — Anti-ICE Mass Protests (2025)
sub: Tens of thousands of protesters filled Los Angeles streets in January–February 2025 to oppose the Trump administration's mass deportation campaign. Demonstrations blocked downtown streets; California Governor Newsom coordinated with local officials on legal challenges.

label: Chicago — Family Separation Rallies (2018)
sub: Massive rallies took place in Chicago in June 2018 to protest the family separation crisis. Thousands gathered outside the ICE field office downtown; organizations coordinated bail funds and legal assistance for detained immigrants.

label: JFK Airport — Muslim Ban / Deportation Protests (2017)
sub: Thousands of protesters descended on JFK International Airport in January 2017 after the Muslim Ban executive order caused detentions of travelers. The airport protests led to a rapid legal challenge; federal judges issued emergency stays nationwide. Event marked the beginning of sustained anti-ICE activism.

label: Washington D.C. — Families Belong Together March (2018)
sub: On June 30, 2018, hundreds of thousands of protesters marched nationwide in the 'Families Belong Together' rallies. The Washington, D.C. march was largest, with crowds estimated at 30,000+ demanding reunification of separated families and an end to family detention.

label: Houston ICE Field Office
sub: ICE Enforcement and Removal Operations field office covering Texas's most populous city. Major operations hub for interior enforcement across the Houston metro area, which has one of the largest undocumented immigrant populations in the U.S.

label: Atlanta ICE ERO Field Office
sub: ICE Enforcement and Removal Operations field office in Atlanta covering the Southeast. Oversees the Stewart Detention Center in Lumpkin and multiple other contracted facilities. Atlanta region consistently among highest-arrest ICE jurisdictions.

label: Phoenix ICE ERO Field Office
sub: ICE field office in Phoenix covering Arizona and Nevada. Oversees Eloy, Florence, and California City detention facilities. Arizona's proximity to the border and historical enforcement operations make Phoenix one of ICE's most active field offices.

label: New Orleans ICE Field Office
sub: ICE field office covering Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Arkansas, and Tennessee. Oversees numerous contracted facilities in Louisiana. The New Orleans AOR holds one of the highest detention populations relative to its immigrant population.

label: California City Detention Facility
sub: GEO Group-operated facility in California City in the Mojave Desert. Far from Los Angeles and detainees' families and attorneys. Significant isolation from legal services. Part of ICE's California detention complex.

label: Otero County Processing Center
sub: LaSalle Corrections-operated facility in Chaparral, New Mexico near the Texas/New Mexico border. One of the key facilities in the El Paso Area of Responsibility. Multiple detainee deaths and DHS oversight reports.

label: Adams County Correctional Center
sub: CoreCivic-operated facility in Natchez, Mississippi used for ICE detainees. Isolated from major immigration courts and legal services in the Deep South. Part of the Louisiana/Mississippi ICE detention cluster.

label: Minneapolis — Hussen v. Noem Class Action
sub: The U.S. District Court for Minnesota where the Hussen v. Noem class action was filed in January 2026 challenging ICE's use of suspicionless stops, warrantless arrests, and racial profiling in the 2025 enforcement surge. Significant test of Fourth Amendment limits on mass immigration enforcement.

label: Caroline Detention Facility
sub: GEO Group-operated facility in Bowling Green, Virginia in ICE's Washington D.C. field office area of responsibility. Holds detainees from the Mid-Atlantic region. Significant legal challenges over access to counsel.

label: Denver — ICE Resistance Protests (2019)
sub: Denver, Colorado was a focal point for ICE resistance in 2019 following ICE's targeting of sanctuary jurisdictions. Protests at Denver's ICE field office; Colorado legislature debated sanctuary legislation. Community organizations organized rapid response networks.

label: Boston — ACLU Challenges ICE Courthouse Arrests
sub: Boston became a flashpoint for the practice of ICE arresting immigrants inside and near state courthouses. Massachusetts courts issued policies against ICE courthouse arrests; the First Circuit ultimately ruled on the issue. Boston advocates organized legal defense networks.

label: Portland, OR — 'No Kings' ICE Facility Breach (2026)
sub: Site of the March 28, 2026 'No Kings' protests where demonstrators breached the front gate of the Portland ICE field office building. Portland Police declared a riot; 3 arrested for criminal mischief and assault. The 9th Circuit had days earlier reversed an injunction limiting federal agents' use of crowd-control weapons at this location.

label: Sacramento — ICE Officer Vehicle Attack (2026)
sub: On March 25, 2026, Xa Lee, a Laotian national with prior felony convictions, rammed his vehicle into an ICE officer during an arrest attempt and fled. DHS publicly announced the attack on March 30, characterizing it as part of a 'disturbing trend of vehicle attacks on ICE officers.' The incident was cited in congressional debates over ICE officer safety legislation.

label: Honolulu, HI — No Kings Protest Assault (2026)
sub: During a 'No Kings' demonstration in Honolulu on March 31, 2026, a 15-year-old was arrested on second-degree assault charges after attacking a man wearing ICE-style gear. Part of the nationwide wave of anti-ICE protests following the March 28 No Kings demonstrations, which organizers estimated drew 8–9 million participants nationwide.

label: Minnesota State Capitol — 'No Kings' Rally (2026)
sub: Site of the largest 'No Kings' protest rally on March 28, 2026, with over 100,000 demonstrators calling attention to ongoing ICE enforcement and the DHS partial government shutdown. Protesters specifically highlighted the continued presence of ~500 ICE agents in Minnesota following Operation Metro Surge, details of which (schools patrolled, agents harassing children) were revealed via police emails published by Bloomberg on March 27.

label: Fort Polk / Fort Johnson, LA — Army Spouse Detained (2026)
sub: Army Staff Sgt. Matthew Blank's wife Annie Yaritza Ramos Alvarado was detained by ICE on April 2, 2026 after the couple visited Fort Polk (officially renamed Fort Johnson in 2023) to register her as a military spouse and begin processing her green card application. She had lived in the U.S. since age 2. Released under GPS monitor April 7 as deportation proceedings continued.

label: Mesa, AZ — ICE Temporary Holding Facility
sub: ICE temporary holding facility in Mesa, Arizona, designed for up to 157 individuals but averaging 513 per day in early 2026 — with peaks of 800 per day. ICE moved detainees out before a congressional oversight visit by Reps. Greg Stanton and Yassamin Ansari in April 2026, then populations rebounded immediately after. The incident drew attention to ICE's resistance to transparency regarding rapidly expanded detention capacity.

label: Fort Bliss / Camp East Montana — Military ICE Detention (2026)
sub: Military detention site at Fort Bliss, Texas used by ICE to house migrants under the Trump administration's expanded use of military facilities for immigration detention. A death at the facility in 2026 was ruled a homicide, contributing to the record pace of ICE custody deaths in FY2026. One of multiple military installations repurposed for immigrant detention as ICE pursued its 100,000-bed expansion goal.

label: South Florida — 'Alligator Alcatraz' Detention (2026)
sub: ICE detention facility in an environmentally sensitive area near the Florida Everglades, informally dubbed 'Alligator Alcatraz.' Built without required environmental impact review, per environmental groups. The 11th Circuit Court of Appeals on April 21, 2026 vacated a preliminary injunction that had halted the facility, ruling the district court 'abused its discretion.' Capacity: up to 1,500 detainees. Environmental plaintiffs (Friends of the Everglades, Center for Biological Diversity, Miccosukee Tribe) continue litigation.

label: U.S. Capitol — Senate $70B ICE Reconciliation Vote
sub: On April 23, 2026 the Senate voted 50-48 to adopt a budget resolution authorizing up to $70 billion in new funding for ICE and Border Patrol via reconciliation — bypassing the 60-vote filibuster threshold. The vote came after DHS was in partial shutdown since February 14 following the fatal shooting of two Minneapolis U.S. citizens by federal agents in January 2026. Only Sens. Rand Paul (KY) and Lisa Murkowski (AK) broke Republican ranks. The resolution advances to the House; President Trump set a June 1 deadline.

label: Kansas City International Airport (KCI) — ICE Air Hub
sub: KCUR/Beacon News investigation (Apr 23, 2026) identified KCI as a major ICE Air deportation and transfer hub: 130 departures and 106 arrivals in 2025; 33 departures and 21 arrivals through March 2026. ICE Air flights expanded to 79 deportation destination countries. Charter airlines used: GlobalX, Key Lime, Omni, Eastern Air Express. A CoreCivic facility in nearby Leavenworth, KS (reopened Mar 2026, 1,000 capacity) intensifies KCI operations. Nationally, ICE Air reached 1,794 flights in March 2026 — a 122% increase year-over-year.
